# Coffee Brewing Basics

## Espresso

Espresso is a concentrated coffee brewed by forcing hot water through finely-ground coffee under high pressure (around 9 bars). A standard double shot uses 18 grams of coffee and yields about 36 grams of liquid in 25 to 30 seconds.

The grind for espresso should be very fine, similar to powdered sugar. If the shot pulls too fast and tastes sour, grind finer. If it pulls too slow and tastes bitter, grind coarser.

t pulls too slow and tastes bitter, grind coarser.

Water temperature should sit between 90 and 96 degrees Celsius. Most home machines preheat the group head; commercial machines hold the boiler at a stable temperature.

## Pour Over

A pour over uses a paper filter and a slow, controlled stream of water. Common devices include the V60, Chemex, and Kalita Wave. The ratio is typically 1 gram of coffee to 16 grams of water.

s typically 1 gram of coffee to 16 grams of water.

Grind size for pour over is medium-fine, similar to table salt. Brew time should be between 3 and 4 minutes total.

## Beans

Coffee beans come from two main species: Arabica and Robusta. Arabica is sweeter and more aromatic, while Robusta is more bitter and contains roughly twice the caffeine.

Beans are best used within four weeks of roasting. Store in an opaque, airtight container at room temperature. Never freeze ground coffee.

# Electric Bicycles Guide

## Motors

Electric bicycles use one of two motor types: hub motors (mounted in the wheel) or mid-drive motors (mounted at the bottom bracket). Mid-drive motors leverage the bike's gears, giving better climbing performance and efficiency, especially on hills.

g performance and efficiency, especially on hills.

Power is measured in watts. In Europe, pedal-assist bikes (EPACs) are limited to 250 watts continuous output and 25 km/h assisted speed. In the United States, Class 1 and Class 2 bikes top out at 20 mph, while Class 3 bikes reach 28 mph.

## Batteries

The battery is the heaviest and most expensive component of an electric bicycle. Capacity is measured in watt-hours (Wh). A typical commuter battery holds between 400 and 700 Wh.

cal commuter battery holds between 400 and 700 Wh.

Range depends on terrain, rider weight, assist level, and wind. A 500 Wh battery delivers roughly 40 to 80 km of range in normal conditions. Lithium-ion batteries lose capacity over time and should be stored at around 50 percent charge when not used for long periods.

## Pedal Assist Levels

not used for long periods.

## Pedal Assist Levels

Most e-bikes offer three to five assist levels. Eco mode extends range, while Turbo or Boost mode prioritizes power. Some bikes also include a walk-assist mode that pushes the bike at walking speed when you are off the saddle.

A torque sensor measures how hard you press the pedals and scales motor output accordingly. A cadence sensor only measures whether you are pedaling, which gives a less natural ride.
